5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Angular applications continue to grow in size. Features like PDF exports, AI integrations, image editing, Excel generation, and advanced analytics often bring large third-party libraries into your application. The problem is that most users may never use those features during a session, yet everyone pays the cost of downloading them.
Angular 22 introduces injectAsync(), a new dependency injection API that lets you lazy load services on demand while preserving Angular's familiar DI experience.
Instead of eagerly including every service in your initial JavaScript bundle, Angular can split heavy services into separate chunks and load them only when they’re actually needed.
In this article, we’ll explore how injectAsync() works, when to use it, complete real-world examples, prefetching, performance considerations, and best practices.
The Problem
Imagine an enterprise dashboard that supports:

• PDF exports

• AI-powered content generation

• Excel reports

• Image compression

• Charts & analytics
